headlines.title: DAM DUG INTO TO PROVE BASE SOUND
headlines.subtitle: Concrete Found Sunk Seven Feet and of Solid Construction.
headlines.fullText:
Charges made by the advisory committee of the Chamber of Commerce that the Lake Worth dam base is unsound were refuted Friday when Commissioner's Grant, Allen and Hanke made an inspection. Superintendent Lord had tunnels dug into the base of the dam, and at each place it was found that the base was sunk more than seven feet and was constructed of solid concrete. The committee charged the dam base was but eighteen inches deep and was not made of solid concrete.
At a recent meeting of the city commission, N. H. Lassiter and other members of the committee declared they had been told that the dam was unsafe.
The investigations made by Superintendent Lord and the commissioners satisfied the commissioners that the structure is absolutely safe.
At the suggestion of Commissioners Grant, Allen and Blanke, Superintendent Lord will obtain samples of the concrete taken from the base and turn them over to the city purchasing agent for tests. This will be done to determine whether the concrete is made according to specifications.

headlines.title: Ft. Worth Progress Praised at Auditorium Dedication
headlines.subtitle: Crowd Attends Ceremony Friday Night-Building Will Be of Vast Benefit to City.
headlines.fullText:
Fort Worth's Chamber of Commerce auditorium, the only one of its kind in the southwest, was dedicated Friday night in an address by R. W. Flournoy. He said this structure placed Fort Worth in the front ranks as the convention city of this southwest, where the greatest artists and associations of the nation would gather at great assemblages.
H. E. Finney, who introduced the speaker, also declared this was the greatest forward step of progress Fort Worth has taken. He said Fort Worth has taken a lead over all her rivals in the Southwest in the completion of the new building. Speaking of the city, with its offices in the basement of the building, he said it now could bring the leaders of every movement of importance to Fort Worth for their deliberations.
Crowd at Ceremony.
The auditorium was almost filled for the occasion and all classes were well represented. The lower floor and the boxes were filled and there were several hundred persons in the balcony.
A resolution offering a vote of thanks to Secretary R. O. McCormack and former President G. H. Clifford of the Chamber of Commerce was offered by W. B. Burton. It was seconded by H. F. Finney and unanimously adopted. The resolution also extended the vote of thanks to all other young men who assisted.
A special orchestra was provided for the first renditions. This brought a hearty applause and a few cheers and yells. The orchestra then played "My Country, 'Tis of Thee." The audience rose and stood until it was finished.
H. E. Finney Talks.
H. E. Finney opened the ceremonies with a short talk.
"We are tonight celebrating two great historical events," he said. "The birthday of a great man, Robbie Burns, and the opening of the first auditorium southwest of Kansas City. Robbie spent his life teaching of love, harmony and co-operation and now it is the living of those principles which has made this building possible. It was built, not by sluggards, but by the young men who gave their energies to it, and the giving of cash by the older fellows.
"This means more for the development and progress of Fort Worth than anything the Chamber of Commerce has done for eight or nine years."
Flournoy Lauds Enterprise.
R. W. Flournoy first complimented the officers of the Chamber of Commerce who conceived the idea for the new auditorium. He said it was a monument to the liberality of the citizenship, men who equipped Fort Worth for great assemblages.
He likened the auditorium to a forum of free speech for civic and sociological movements. He said it would be open to the representatives of any respectable interests at all times.
"There now are more pressing problems, economically, sociologically and politically than ever before in the world's history," he continued. "There is a spirit of unrest throughout this nation and here things may be discussed. A following will enter here and strife will find a place among the people of this country. Great truths and great falsehoods will be uttered, but finally the voice of reason will be raised to declare the truth and to protect the equilibrium of American thought. The outlook was more hopeful for the future of American life and national strife in America. It is the freedom in these gatherings, but it is safe when given free thought.
"In the name of Fort Worth, its great, broad future, to the spirit of the time I dedicate this splendid home of liberal arts and truth."
Musical Program.
Miss Gladys Moody opened the musical program which followed with the rendition of the "Hungarian Rhapsody No. 6," by Liszt.
Carl Venth gave a violin solo, "Fantasie," one of his own compositions.
Mrs. Frank Lidden and Mrs. Roger C. Agar gave a duet from "Il Trovatore," "Two Hopes was written by Mrs. Lidden's father, who was her accompanist, and a reading on "Matrimony" by Mrs. Hunter Gardner, followed.
Mrs. Pearl Calhoun Davis and Prof. C. Agar gave a duet from "Il Trovatore."
Three Majestic acts also assisted in the entertainment, through courtesy of Karl Hoblitzelle, head of the circuit. They were Will H. Fox, trick pianist in his "Musical Monologue," Halligan and Sykes, a team act and Jane Adair, formerly of Adair and Hickey. She is now the wife of James Thompson, appearing on this week's bill.

headlines.title: FINANCIAL TONE BETTER; SENDS STOCKS UPWARD
headlines.subtitle: Volume of Business Increased During Week-Money Rate Reduced.
headlines.fullText:
NEW YORK, Jan. 24. There was a marked improvement in the financial district this week. The volume of business increased to a greater extent than at any previous time within many months. The change in the securities market reflected distinct betterment in fundamentals. President Wilson's message to congress regarding anti-trust legislation relieved the market of uncertainty as to the attitude of the administration and the tone of the message was regarded as encouraging.
Improvement in monetary conditions was shown by the accumulation of money funds at this center, and in lower discount rates of foreign banks. The change for the better in the investment market was indicated by the striking success of New York state's $51,000,000 issue of 4 1/2 per cent bonds and their resale to successful bidders at a premium. The outlook was more hopeful for future business.

headlines.title: Missing Will and Other Papers Found
headlines.fullText: FAIRFAX COURTHOUSE, Va., Jan. 24.—E. P. Eggleston of Connecticut has returned to the clerk of the county court the will of Martha Washington. Eggleston stated he did not know until he had read accounts of efforts to recover the Martha Washington will now in the possession of J. P. Morgan in New York.

headlines.title: SIEGEL REORGANIZATION PLAN AGREED UPON
headlines.subtitle: Boston Store Will Be Sold or Closed. Creditors Will Be Protected.
headlines.fullText:
NEW YORK, Jan. 24.—Reorganization plans to protect creditors of the Henry Siegel stores in this city and Boston were agreed upon today, according to lawyers representing interests involved in the bankruptcy proceedings.
Plans tentatively agreed upon provide for the treatment of all creditors as one class, and for two corporations to take over the Fourteenth street store and the Simpson-Crawford Company store, and the sale or closing, so far as the Siegel interests are concerned, of the Siegel store in Boston.
Payment in cash is to be made to depositors in the banking house of Henry Siegel & Co. of from 25 to 35 per cent of their claims, the balance to be paid in notes to be secured by stock in the new corporation.
Payment of 10 to 15 per cent of the mercantile claims and those of commercial banks for loans, will be made in cash, the balance being paid in stock of the new company.

headlines.title: PLANS FOR FEDERAL OIL PIPE LINE DISCUSSED
headlines.subtitle: Proposed System Would Run From Oklahoma to Gulf to Supply Navy.
headlines.fullText:
WASHINGTON, Jan. 24.—Plans for a government oil pipe line from the Oklahoma oil fields to the Gulf of Mexico to supply fuel for the navy, were discussed at a conference today between Cato Sells, commissioner of Indian affairs, and Lieutenant Commander Boyd of the navy department.
Hearings will be held here of those interested and a number of Oklahomans are preparing to express their views. The commission will visit the oil fields and also the Gulf coast. Every phase of the subject is to be considered before a decision is reached as to whether government control will be recommended.

headlines.title: Municipal Store Opens in Chicago; Others Possible
headlines.fullText:
CHICAGO, Jan. 24.—Chicago's first municipal store, opened with an appropriation of $25,000, and at which groceries are to be sold at cost to the householders, was opened today.
If the market proves a success, meats and wearing apparel also will be sold at other stores opened.
"The municipal markets never will be opened in 'pauper neighborhoods,'" said Joseph Meyer, county agent, who is in charge of the enterprise. "The county agent's office was created to give foodstuffs and clothing to persons without funds. The money markets are to help working people practice economy by purchasing their provisions at cost."

headlines.title: ARMOUR OFFICIALS TESTIMONY SOUGHT
headlines.subtitle: Mandamus Suit Will Be Filed Against Vice President of Refrigerating Car Lines.
headlines.fullText:
CHICAGO, Jan. 24.—Patrick J. Farrell, solicitor for the Interstate Commerce Commission, began preparations today to file a mandamus suit against Ellis T. Robbins, vice president of the Armour Car Lines, to compel him to give information to the commission concerning the affairs of the corporation.
At a hearing Thursday night before Commissioner Charles C. McChord, Ellis refused to testify relative to his company's financial relations with Armour & Co. and its income from car rentals. The petition asking for a mandamus will be forwarded to Washington for approval and probably will not be filed with the court in this city until next week.
The commission's investigation continued today with Edward W. Fitz-Simmons, president of the company, and other members of the commission.

headlines.title: ORIENT OF TEXAS ELECTS OFFICIALS
headlines.subtitle: Edward Dickinson named President and N. J. O'Brien First Vice President.
headlines.fullText:
SAN ANGELO, Texas, Jan. 24.—Officers of the Kansas City, Mexico & Orient railway in Texas were elected yesterday.
Edward Dickinson was named president; N. J. O'Brien, first vice president and general manager; M. L. Mertz, second vice president and treasurer; H. S. Garrett, third vice president, general attorney, secretary and claim agent; H. P. Parker, chief engineer; J. H. Rose, auditor; J. P. O'Donnell, general traffic manager and freight claim agent; W. L. Karnes, assistant secretary.

headlines.title: COLQUITT HINTS AT THIRD TERM RACE
headlines.subtitle: Governor Also Intimates That He Will Remove to Dallas When Office Expires.
headlines.fullText:
DALLAS, Jan. 24.—Governor Colquitt, who was here yesterday in an interview, hinted that there was a possibility he would make the race for a third term. He also said that after his term expired he may remove to Dallas.
"I am being asked by friends to run again," he said. "I have no present intentions of running but I haven't promised that I will not be a candidate."

headlines.title: POTATO QUARANTINE REGULATION MODIFIED
headlines.subtitle: Countries Can Make Extensive Laws or Prohibit Importation of Crop to U. S. When Not Home Grown.
headlines.fullText:
WASHINGTON, Jan. 24.—Regulations issued recently prohibiting the importation of potatoes from Canada and Europe to guard against the introduction of potato diseases were modified today by the Federal horticultural board.
The previous order, which would have required countries not affected by the quarantine, including Bermuda, to pass their own quarantine acts, was changed to permit the secretary of agriculture to waive this requirement in favor of forbidding the importation in this country of potatoes not home-grown.
